preFix.title: Caseous lymphadenitis in sheep
preFix.subtitle: What the lumps under the skin are, why CLA is worth taking seriously, and how to manage it in a small flock.
preFix.excerpt: Caseous lymphadenitis (CLA) causes firm, painless abscesses in the lymph nodes of sheep and goats. The abscesses contain a thick, odourless, chalky or caseous pus and the causative bacterium, Corynebacterium pseudotuberculosis. There is no licensed treatment in the UK. Management is about reducing spread to the rest of the flock.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb: Caseous Lymphadenitis factsheet. Moredun Research Institute CLA guidance notes. APHA disease surveillance.

content.text: Caseous lymphadenitis is one of the most common chronic conditions in UK sheep flocks and is very frequently introduced through purchased stock.
CLA

abscesses are easy to miss until they are large enough to feel or see under the fleece, and by the time one drains, the bacteria have been shed into the environment. A small flock can harbour the infection for years before a keeper notices.
What it looks like
The abscesses caused by
Corynebacterium pseudotuberculosis

appear most commonly at the prescapular node (in front of the shoulder), the parotid node (near the jaw), the prefemoral node (in the flank), and the popliteal node (behind the knee). The lumps are firm, cool, and painless when you press them. They grow slowly over months and may eventually soften and rupture, draining thick
caseous pus
. Internal abscesses (on the lungs, liver, or spinal cord) are invisible from outside and usually only found at slaughter.
Why it matters
Infected carcasses are condemned or partially condemned at the abattoir. This represents a significant financial loss for finishers.
The bacteria survive in soil for many months, potentially years. Once a paddock is contaminated, it remains a source of infection.
The infection spreads through contaminated dipping baths, shared shearing equipment, and any wound that lets bacteria enter a new host.
There is no licensed treatment or vaccine in the UK. Management is containment.
Managing an affected flock

Never lance or open an abscess in the field. The pus contains viable bacteria and will contaminate soil and equipment. If the abscess ruptures naturally, collect and dispose of the exudate (double-bag and bin or bury deeply away from grazing areas).
Identify affected animals. Mark them and track which animals have active abscesses.

Shear affected animals last, with thoroughly cleaned equipment, or cull before shearing. The shearing wound is the primary entry point; shearing a sheep with a draining abscess and then moving to the next animal with the same handpiece spreads the infection.
Buy only from accredited CLA-free flocks, or quarantine all purchased stock for six weeks and palpate lymph nodes before mixing with your flock.
Consider culling persistently affected animals rather than carrying them.
Zoonotic risk

CLA can infect humans through open wounds or through the mucous membranes of the eye or mouth, causing a condition called Oedematous skin disease or, in rare cases, systemic illness. Wear protective gloves when handling sheep with active abscesses and avoid any contact between abscess pus and open skin or eyes.
